title: Electric potential energy: why separating charges takes finite energy
scenario: Two opposite point charges ($q_1 = 2\ \mu$C, $q_2 = 2\ \mu$C) are held a separation $R = 2$ cm apart and attract with $F = kq_1q_2/r^2$. How much energy does it take to pull them fully apart — and why is that energy *finite*? Drag the cursor outward and watch the potential energy climb toward a ceiling as the area under $1/r^2$ converges. This is the electric twin of escape energy: the same inverse-square area, charge in place of mass.

assumptions.claim: Point charges (or spherical charge distributions), so outside them the force is $kq_1q_2/r^2$.
Opposite charges (attraction); $q_1, q_2$ are magnitudes, and the energy shown is the work *supplied* to separate them. The potential energy is taken zero at infinite separation.
The pull is quasi-static (no kinetic-energy bookkeeping — this is the potential energy alone); $k \approx 8.99\times10^{9}$ N·m²/C².

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
steps.prose: The algebra-based course hands you the point-charge energy $kq_1q_2/r$ as a formula, and for a uniform field (parallel plates) the energy $qEd$ — a constant force times a distance. It can compute the number but cannot show that the energy is an *area* under the Coulomb force, or why pulling a bound pair completely apart takes only a *finite* amount of energy when the force reaches out forever.

steps.label: Electric PE is the area under the force–distance curve
steps.prose: The energy to pull two attracting charges apart is the integral of the Coulomb force over distance — the area under the $F$–$r$ curve. For a constant force (a uniform field) this is a rectangle ($qEd$); for the inverse-square force of point charges it is the area under a falling curve.

steps.label: Evaluate the inverse-square integral
steps.prose: The area under $1/r^2$ from $R$ to $r$ is $kq_1q_2(1/R - 1/r)$. Its slope at any separation is $kq_1q_2/r^2 = F(r)$ — the force is the rate at which the potential energy accumulates. This is the *same* inverse-square area as gravity, with charge in place of mass.

steps.label: The separation energy is finite — the area converges
steps.prose: Push the cursor outward and the energy rises toward a *finite* ceiling, even as $r\to\infty$: the area under $1/r^2$ converges. That ceiling is the binding energy — the energy needed to completely separate the pair — and SymPy proves the limit. A constant force would give an infinite area; the inverse square does not. (To bring point charges all the way *together*, $r\to 0$, does diverge — the convergent end is infinity, not contact.)

proof.heading: Electric PE is the area under the Coulomb force curve — and the area to infinity converges to a finite separation energy.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{d}{dr}\Delta U = F$; check $\Delta U = \int_R^r F\,dr'$; check $\lim_{r\to\infty}\Delta U = kq_1q_2/R$; collapse the uniform-field case to $qEd$

checks.claim: The PE's slope is the force: $U'(r) = F(r)$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The accumulated PE is the area: $\Delta U(r) = \int_R^r F\,dr'$.
The area to infinity converges: the energy to fully separate the pair $\to kq_1q_2/R$ is finite as $r\to\infty$.
In a uniform field $F\approx qE$ is constant, so $U \approx qEd$ — the area is a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: It takes infinite energy to completely separate two bound charges, since the electric force reaches out forever.
misconception.refuted_by: The force does reach out forever, but it *weakens* as $1/r^2$ — and the area under $1/r^2$ from $R$ to infinity is *finite*. That finite area is the binding energy $kq_1q_2/R$: pull the charges to infinity and the work you must supply converges to it, exactly as a rocket needs only finite energy $GMm/R$ to escape a planet. (Bringing point charges all the way *together*, $r\to 0$, is the end that diverges — not the separation.) Drag the cursor out: the potential energy rises toward a ceiling, not without bound.

practice.asks: the energy to separate the charges completely
practice.prompt: Two $+2\,\mu$C charges sit $R = 2$ cm apart. How much energy is needed to pull them infinitely far apart?

choices.misconception: That is the work to double the separation ($kq_1q_2/2R$). To pull them fully apart (to infinity) takes $kq_1q_2/R$.
That is the electrostatic force $kq_1q_2/R^2$ (in newtons). The energy is $kq_1q_2/R$ (in joules) — one fewer power of $r$.
